Chapter 3
History taking and examination
Aidan Raftery and Lianne Foxall
Guinea pigs are naturally nervous animals and instinctively 
act on the flight response. This means that adequate 
restraint and suitable transportation are important both 
en route to and inside the veterinary practice. Their prey 
instincts mean that in many cases clinical signs of illness 
are hidden or not noticed until the animal is very unwell, 
and vague signs are often not noticed by the owner. 
Detailed history taking and systematic examination are 
therefore vital for making a diagnosis and appropriate 
treatment decisions. The clinician must be familiar with 
normal anatomy to recognize abnormalities on initial 
examination (see ‘Clinical examination’, below, and chap-
ters covering specific body systems).
Transport
The guinea pig should be transported to the veterinary 
practice in an appropriately sized secure carrier that has 
a lid and is easily accessible without being dismantled  An appropriate transport carrier for a guinea pig, with 
3.1
(Figure 3.1). Commercial cat carriers are suitable, although  suitable bedding/hide material provided.
any with a wire base should be avoided as they may cause 
foot injuries. The carrier should be made of a material that

is sturdy and cannot be chewed, to avoid the risk of the 
animal escaping. It should be filled with hay or fleece

material to provide visual security and a hiding space.

Chapter 3 • History taking and examination
suspicion of dental disease or calcium deposits in the 
urine. See Chapter 2 for further information on the appro-
priate diet for guinea pigs.
Given the social dynamics of guinea pigs, animals 
kept in large groups may encounter bullying leading to 
traumatic injuries and reduced access to food. This may 
present as weight loss, coat changes or infections sec-
ondary to chronic stress and immunosuppression. It may 
be beneficial to assess whether sufficient sources of 
water, food and shelter are provided for the number of 
individuals in the group, as this can reduce the likelihood 
of bullying.
If multiple animals in the group are affected, it is pru-
dent to formulate a treatment plan to include all affected 
individuals, for example, to prevent transmission and 
reinfection in cases of respiratory infections. In some 
cases, such as mite infestations, it may be best to treat 
non-affected guinea pigs as well. In some situations 
where there is an infectious or transmissible disease, 
there may be unaffected carriers, which should also be 
accounted for in the treatment plan. Where non-infectious  3.4 Suitable restraint of a guinea pig for examination.
health problems are present, such as hypovitaminosis C 
(scurvy), there may be variation in the presenting signs  1. Cachexic – there is extreme weight loss and

and have minimal fat covering. The abdomen is

Your pet is likely to be obese and this can have serious medical implications. Being overweight is unhealthy 
for pets as it can lead to a shortened life-span, high blood pressure, heart disease, arthritis, cystitis and 
OBESE 5 other health complications. Speak to your local vet for advice and a thorough health check-up. The vet will 
look for any underlying medical reasons as to why your pet may be too heavy. If there are no underlying 
health issues, a weight loss programme will probably be individually developed for your pet and should 
include diet and lifestyle changes.
www.ukpetfood.org
For more information on the Guinea Pig Size-O-Meter and tips on how to prevent weight gain visit www.ukpetfood.org. In addition to providing 
useful tips on how to keep your pet healthy and happy, a team of veterinary nutrition experts are on hand to answer your pet nutrition questions 
in the ‘Ask the Expert’ section.
A body condition chart for guinea pigs. The recommended diet for a healthy pet guinea pig is ad libitum hay and/or grass, forage,  
3.5
a large cup of leafy green vegetables once or twice daily and a small ration of a complete uniform pelleted diet that is fortified with 
stabilized vitamin C. See Chapter 2 for further information about nutrition in guinea pigs.
